sections.content:

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: Level 1 of a grid puzzle. The board appears to have a black background with several colored objects: yellow and blue cross/plus shapes, and small gray squares with colored centers. Need to understand the layout and goal.
- Plan: First inspect the board structure via segmentation, then determine what actions do.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check what changed after the RIGHT action by comparing the before and after frames.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after RIGHT action
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

before_seg = before.segmentation['nodes']
after_seg = after.segmentation['nodes']

print(f"Before: {len(before_seg)} nodes, After: {len(after_seg)} nodes")

# Find the white pixel in both frames
before_white = [n for n in before_seg if n['color'] == 'W']
after_white = [n for n in after_seg if n['color'] == 'W']

print(f"Before white nodes: {len(before_white)}")
for n in before_white:
    b = n['boundary']
    print(f"  pos: ({b[0][0]}, {b[0][1]}), pixels={n['pixels']}")

print(f"After white nodes: {len(after_white)}")
for n in after_white:
    b = n['boundary']
    print(f"  pos: ({b[0][0]}, {b[0][1]}), pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check if anything else changed
before_hashes = {n['hash']: n for n in before_seg}
after_hashes = {n['hash']: n for n in after_seg}

# Compare node counts by color
from collections import Counter
before_colors = Counter(n['color'] for n in before_seg)
after_colors = Counter(n['color'] for n in after_seg)
print(f"\nBefore colors: {dict(before_colors)}")
print(f"After colors: {dict(after_colors)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The white pixel moved from (45, 36) to (45, 39) - it moved 3 cells to the right. A new 'w' (light gray) node appeared, likely a trail left behind. The white pixel is a cursor that moves with arrow keys, and it seems to leave a trail.
Let me understand the movement better - it moved 3 cells right, not 1. This might mean it moves until it hits something (the blue cross arm). Let me explore more to understand the mechanics.
The goal might be to navigate the white cursor to collect or interact with the colored target squares. Let me try moving in different directions to understand the movement pattern.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The white pixel moved from (45,36) to (45,39) - moved 3 cells right
# A new light gray 'w' pixel appeared (trail)
# Let me check the new node and understand movement

after_seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
w_nodes = [n for n in after_seg if n['color'] == 'w']
print(f"Light gray trail nodes: {len(w_nodes)}")
for n in w_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"  bounds: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")

# The white pixel moved along the blue horizontal bar (row 45)
# It seems to move until it hits the edge of the blue cross
# Let me try moving LEFT to go back
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
